original_abstract: Training semantic parsers for large-scale knowledge bases like Freebase typically requires expensive annotations of logical forms. In this paper, we present a method for learning a semantic parser using only question-answer pairs, making it more scalable to open-domain data. To address the challenge of navigating the vast space of logical predicates, we introduce two key strategies: first, we establish a coarse alignment between natural language phrases and predicates by leveraging a large text corpus and the knowledge base itself; second, we introduce a "bridging" operation that infers implicit relations by generating predicates compatible with adjacent logical components. We evaluate our approach on a standard benchmark, where our system achieves 62% accuracy, outperforming the previous state-of-the-art despite using weaker supervision. Furthermore, we introduce WEBQUESTIONS, a new dataset of questions collected via search engine queries and answered by crowdsourced workers, demonstrating that our parser effectively handles more diverse and realistic natural language challenges.
humanized_abstract: Training semantic parsers for large-scale knowledge bases such as Freebase has generally required expensive logical form annotations. We present a method for semantic parser training from only question-answer pairs, making the approach more applicable to open-domain question answering scenarios. We address the predicate space explosion issue with two novel ideas: 1) we train a coarse parser that maps linguistic phrases to predicates in Freebase using a large amount of text and Freebase itself as training data; 2) we train a “bridging” parser that finds implicit relationships between phrases by predicting predicates that can link any two logical forms. Our parser achieves 62% accuracy on our semantic parser evaluation dataset, while using less supervision than prior work. We also introduce a new dataset named WEBQUESTIONS that consists of a large set of crowdsourced question answers to search engine queries. The results show that our parser can indeed handle the great variability of natural language expressions present in real-world human queries.

Our parser achieves 62% accuracy on our semantic parser evaluation dataset, while using less supervision than prior work. We also introduce a new dataset named WEBQUESTIONS that consists of a large set of crowdsourced question answers to search engine queries. The results show that our parser can indeed handle the great variability of natural language expressions present in real-world human queries.
